---
title: "Multi-Axis Speech Similarity via Factor-Partitioned Embeddings"
bibliography: sst.bib
keywords:
  - speech representation learning
  - contrastive learning
  - knowledge distillation
  - disentangled representations
  - speaker embeddings
  - multi-attribute retrieval
---

## Abstract

Speech encodes multiple simultaneous attributes — linguistic content, speaker identity, dialect, gender — that conventional single-vector embeddings conflate.
We present a factor-partitioned embedding framework that maps each utterance into a single vector whose subspaces correspond to distinct axes of variation.
A shared acoustic encoder (WavLM) feeds per-axis linear projection heads, each trained via distillation from a specialist teacher or a contrastive objective over shared-label pairs.
The resulting embeddings support attribute-conditioned retrieval: similarity is computed as a signed weighted sum over per-axis cosine scores, allowing retrieval that jointly considers what was said and how it was said — or that explicitly suppresses one attribute to surface another.
We evaluate on cross-corpus retrieval using the OSR and rehasp corpora, which share the Harvard sentence prompts [@rothauser1969ieee], demonstrating that signed axis weighting can suppress same-speaker bias and surface semantically matched utterances across recording conditions.

## Introduction

Speech, in contrast with text, is inherently variable.
We never say the same thing the same way twice: different speakers say the same thing differently, and even the same speaker says the same thing differently at different times, due to a variety of factors such as mood, context, environment, and communicative intent.
Beyond linguistic content, speech reveals much about the speaker: most fundamentally, it reveals their identity, gender, and even health [@nolan1995can].
These attributes are often intertwined, and it can be difficult to disentangle them.

The use case most closely tied with similarity learning is retrieval.
In a multi-field database search, a better match on the keywords in the title might be expected to rank higher despite a mismatch in the author field; the same logic applies to speech, where a query might demand semantic similarity while suppressing speaker identity as a ranking factor.
Many speech datasets contain overlap in the prompts presented to speakers, which presents an opportunity to evaluate the feasibility of such attribute-conditioned similarity.

<!-- this is not accurate; none of these datasets include the Harvard sentences -->
<!--
The Harvard sentences [@rothauser1969ieee], a set of phonetically balanced sentences widely used in speech research, appear across multiple corpora (CMU ARCTIC [@kominek2004cmu], VCTK [@yamagishi2019vctk], and others), enabling cross-corpus retrieval evaluation where the correct match is known by shared prompt identity.
-->

We introduce a factor-partitioned embedding framework for speech that learns a single embedding space partitioned into subspaces corresponding to different attributes.
Given an utterance, the model produces a concatenated embedding vector whose sub-regions are specialised for different attributes: what was said (semantic content), who said it (speaker identity), and how it was said (dialect, gender, etc.).
Similarity is then a vector rather than a scalar, enabling retrieval that is simultaneously sensitive to multiple axes of variation.
While speech embeddings have been studied extensively, most approaches produce a single similarity measure that conflates multiple attributes.
To our knowledge, controllable multi-axis similarity search for speech has not previously been explored.

## Related Work

### Speech representation learning

Self-supervised models such as wav2vec 2.0 [@baevski2020wav2vec], HuBERT [@hsu2021hubert], and WavLM [@chen2022wavlm] learn general-purpose speech representations by masked prediction over audio.
These models capture a mixture of linguistic, speaker, and prosodic information within a single representation, making it difficult to control which attributes drive downstream similarity.

Speaker embeddings such as x-vectors [@snyder2018x] address this for the speaker axis specifically, but do not carry semantic content.

### Acoustic word embeddings and query-by-example retrieval

Acoustic word embeddings (AWEs) map fixed-length audio segments to vectors such that phonetically similar segments are nearby, enabling query-by-example spoken term detection without transcription [@kamper2016acousticwordembeddings; @he2017multiviewrecurrentneuralacoustic; @chung16_interspeech].
The retrieval evaluation paradigm — retrieve audio items matching a spoken query — motivates the cross-corpus setup used here, though at the utterance level rather than the word or segment level.
AWE models optimise a single acoustic similarity notion; our work extends this to multiple simultaneous axes, allowing retrieval to be steered toward semantic content, speaker identity, or other properties independently.

### Sentence transformers and multi-notion similarity

[@reimers2019sentence] introduced Sentence-BERT, which adapts BERT-family models to produce fixed-size sentence embeddings suitable for semantic similarity via contrastive training [@oord2018representation].
The SentenceTransformers library generalises this pipeline to arbitrary encoder–pooler–projector stacks.
We adapt this pipeline to speech by replacing the text encoder with an acoustic encoder (WavLM or similar) and introducing multiple attribute-specific projection heads.

Conditional Similarity Networks [@veit2017conditional] are the closest conceptual prior for our retrieval approach: they learn a single embedding space partitioned into subspaces corresponding to different similarity notions, and select or reweight subspaces at query time via a mask or weight vector.
Our work extends this idea to speech in three respects: (1) application to the speech modality, (2) signed weighting rather than binary masking — including negative weights for suppression — and (3) axis supervision via teacher distillation rather than triplet supervision.

The semantic axis is an instance of speech-to-text retrieval alignment: the speech encoder is trained to produce embeddings that agree with a text-side teacher, enabling content-based retrieval without relying on ASR at inference time.
This pattern has been explored in SpeechDPR [@tseng2022speechdpr] and SpeechRAG [@vo2024speechrag], which align speech encoders to frozen text dense retrievers.
CLAP [@elizalde2023clap] takes a related approach at the audio level, training a joint audio–text embedding space via contrastive learning over audio clips paired with natural-language descriptions.
A model trained on CLAP embeddings serves as a natural comparison for our semantic axis, since both target content-based similarity without relying on ASR.
<!-- TODO: verify SpeechDPR/SpeechRAG bib entries -->

### Disentangled speech representations

Our work has a degree of superficial similarity to work on disentangled representation learning; our goals, however, are more modest.
Rather than enforcing factor independence within the encoder, we focus on supervised learning of multiple relational axes and train per-axis projection heads that induce distinct similarity geometries over a shared frozen acoustic representation space.

The earliest work on sequential disentanglement used factorised hierarchical VAEs (FHVAEs) [@hsu2017unsupervised], which separate "slow" sequence-level factors (speaker, channel, noise) from "fast" segment-level factors (phonetic content) via time-scale structure — without any factor labels.
This established the foundational insight that content and speaker information operate on different time scales and can be separated structurally.

SpeechFlow [@qian2020unsupervised] frames this decomposition explicitly: "speech information can be roughly decomposed into four components: language content, timbre, pitch, and rhythm."
These four components map naturally onto axes that a retrieval system might wish to control independently.
SpeechFlow separates them using an information bottleneck that restricts the bandwidth available to each encoder branch.
ContentVec [@qian2022contentvec] incorporated speaker disentanglement directly into the SSL pre-training objective, showing that masked prediction combined with speaker conditioning can yield representations where the content axis carries substantially less speaker information.

[@lu2023speechtriplenet] extend content/timbre separation to three factors by adding prosody, using a VAE with restricted channel capacity to force disentanglement without explicit supervision.
Their approach is generative and unsupervised; ours is discriminative and supervision-guided, using pre-trained teacher models to provide independent training signal for each axis.
The key difference: they restrict information flow structurally (β-VAE [@higgins2017beta] bottleneck); we provide axis-specific objectives derived from teachers whose representations are known to carry specific information.

A parallel line uses contrastive and adversarial objectives rather than generative bottlenecks to separate content from speaker.
CTVC [@deng2024ctvc] trains a content encoder with a phoneme-level contrastive loss — same-phoneme frames are attracted, boundary frames repelled — while a gradient reversal layer strips speaker identity from the content representation.
A separate speaker encoder is trained by maximising mutual information between embeddings of randomly drawn segments of the same utterance, enforcing that speaker representation is time-invariant within an utterance.
The mechanism is discriminative rather than generative, which is closer in spirit to our own approach, though the goal remains voice conversion rather than controllable retrieval.

A consistent finding across both generative and discriminative approaches is that factor leakage is hard to eliminate completely: adversarial removal can reduce leakage under closed-set conditions but residual speaker information often persists under stronger open-set evaluation.
Our approach does not attempt to eliminate leakage within the encoder; instead it tolerates it and compensates by training axis-specific projection heads that induce the desired similarity geometry, scaling to any axis for which a competent teacher exists.

### Structured and hierarchical representations

An alternative to explicit disentanglement training is to exploit the natural organisation of information in hierarchical representations.
SpeechTokenizer [@zhang2024speechtokenizer] demonstrates that in a residual vector quantisation (RVQ) hierarchy, the first codebook layer organises representations around semantic/phonetic content while later layers carry speaker-specific and paralinguistic detail — without any explicit disentanglement objective.
This suggests that structured representations emerge from hierarchical compression alone.

Our approach occupies a middle position: we impose explicit per-axis objectives, but rely on teacher distillation rather than reconstruction, and produce a single concatenated vector rather than a token stream.

### Distillation into partitioned spaces

MiniLM [@wang2020minilm] distils large transformers into smaller ones via self-attention transfer; we similarly distil teacher embeddings into fixed-size subspaces, but align subspaces across axes rather than across model depths.

## Method

### Architecture

The model follows the SentenceTransformers pipeline:

1. **Acoustic encoder**: a frozen or fine-tuned HuggingFace audio model (default: WavLM-base-plus [@chen2022wavlm]) maps raw waveform frames to a sequence of hidden states.
2. **Pooling**: mean pooling over the frame sequence produces a single vector.
3. **Multi-axis projection**: a set of linear projection heads maps the pooled representation into per-axis subspaces. Each head produces an L2-normalised embedding of a fixed dimension.

The per-axis dimensions are chosen to match the output dimensionality of the corresponding teacher:

| Axis | Teacher | Dim |
|------|---------|-----|
| semantic | all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | 384 |
| speaker\_id | WavLM-base-plus x-vector | 512 |
| dialect | dialect classifier | 12 |
| gender | gender classifier | 2 |

<!-- Note: for categorical axes (dialect, gender), distilling from class logits and using cosine similarity in a 2- or 12-dimensional space is problematic. Prefer distilling from the penultimate (pre-softmax) embedding of the teacher, or training a supervised contrastive objective on a larger learned subspace. -->

The full embedding is the concatenation of all axis projections, although similarity can also be computed directly from individual axis embeddings for axis-specific retrieval.

### Training

Each axis is trained independently using one of three signal sources:

- **Distillation**: a pre-computed teacher embedding is aligned to the projected subspace via cosine loss. An orthogonal alignment matrix is learned when the teacher and projection dimensions differ.
- **Explicit positives**: pairs of utterances known to share an attribute are contrasted using InfoNCE [@oord2018representation].
- **In-batch label matching**: utterances sharing a label within a batch are treated as positives in a supervised contrastive objective.

Batch sampling attempts to minimise label collisions across axes in order to reduce false negatives in the contrastive objective.

### Evaluation: cross-corpus retrieval

Existing evaluation frameworks do not directly measure multi-axis similarity.
Benchmarks such as SUPERB evaluate speech embeddings under individual task metrics (e.g., speaker verification EER, content WER), but not controllable combinations.
MSEB [@heigold2025massive] provides large-scale retrieval evaluation across diverse sound and speech categories, but targets single-axis similarity rather than controllable multi-axis combinations.
The goal of this work is to demonstrate retrieval whose preference can be steered by axis weighting — a behaviour no existing benchmark is designed to measure.
The goal of the experiments is therefore not to establish a definitive benchmark, but to demonstrate the feasibility of controllable multi-axis similarity.

We construct a controlled retrieval task using datasets with overlapping prompts.
OSR and rehasp both record the Harvard sentences [@rothauser1969ieee], providing ground-truth matches by prompt identity.
We evaluate on a mixed index containing both corpora, where the same speaker reads the same sentence multiple times across datasets.
Because the index contains repeated recordings of the same sentence by the same speaker, naive semantic similarity tends to retrieve same-speaker variants before cross-corpus matches.
In this setting, retrieval is evaluated cross-corpus: same-corpus hits (same speaker, different recording) are treated as distractors rather than correct matches.

Because the goal is to support controllable similarity rather than optimise a single metric, evaluation focuses on whether axis weighting changes retrieval behaviour in the expected direction.

**Metrics**: Precision@k, where a hit is counted as correct if it (a) comes from a different corpus than the query, and (b) reads the same sentence.
P@1 reports whether the top overall result is a correct cross-corpus match; P@k (k > 1) reports whether a correct cross-corpus match appears within the top-k cross-corpus results.

**Preference-flip test**: A stronger demonstration of the mechanism is to show that retrieval preference *reverses* when the sign of a weight is flipped.
For each query, the index contains items in four categories: (same sentence, same speaker), (same sentence, different speaker), (different sentence, same speaker), and (different sentence, different speaker).
Under positive `speaker_id` weight, same-speaker matches should dominate; under negative weight, cross-speaker matches reading the same sentence should rise.
We report the mean rank of each category under each weight setting.

### Axis weighting at inference

At inference, per-axis similarities are combined with signed weights.
All axis embeddings are L2-normalised prior to similarity computation:

$$\text{sim}(a, b) = \sum_i w_i \cdot \cos(\mathbf{e}^{(i)}_a, \mathbf{e}^{(i)}_b)$$

A negative weight for an axis repels embeddings that are similar on that axis.
In the mixed-index setting, a negative `speaker_id` weight suppresses same-speaker hits and surfaces cross-corpus matches.

## Datasets

<!-- TODO: add proper citations for OSR and rehasp -->

- **CMU ARCTIC** [@kominek2004cmu]: ~1100 utterances per speaker, 7 speakers, recording-studio quality. Used primarily for training the semantic and speaker teachers rather than for the cross-corpus retrieval evaluation.
- **VCTK** [@yamagishi2019vctk]: ~400 utterances per speaker, 110 speakers, various accents. Also used for teacher training; provides accent and gender labels.
- **Google Britain and Ireland (GBI)**: read-speech recordings from speakers across Britain and Ireland; provides dialect labels used as supervision for the dialect axis.
- **OSR** (Open Speech Repository): a collection of speech recordings for testing Voice over IP (VoIP) applications. The English subset contains recordings of the Harvard sentences [@rothauser1969ieee] by 3 British speakers and 5 American speakers (~720 utterances).
- **rehasp** [@henter14_interspeech]: a single speaker reading the same Harvard sentences multiple times (~1200 utterances), enabling study of within-speaker variability.
- **TSP Speech Database**: Harvard sentences recorded by 24 speakers; permissive BSD licence. A strong candidate for multi-speaker cross-corpus evaluation. <!-- TODO: add TSP citation -->

Common Voice [@ardila2020common] would be the most natural large-scale candidate for this kind of evaluation: it is crowd-sourced, covers a wide range of speakers and accents, and many of its validated sentences appear across multiple contributors, providing the prompt-level overlap that makes ground-truth retrieval possible.
However, the English subset of Common Voice has not been publicly available since mid-2025, following changes to the dataset's distribution terms, and no confirmed timeline for its re-release has been announced.
Some smaller-language subsets have begun to reappear, but English remains unavailable at the time of writing.
The evaluation presented here uses OSR and rehasp as a principled alternative: both corpora record the same Harvard sentence set, giving exact prompt-level ground truth without depending on the transcript overlap that Common Voice would provide probabilistically.
The controlled scale of these corpora is a limitation in terms of speaker diversity, but it allows the preference-flip mechanism to be evaluated under conditions where the correct answer is unambiguous.

## Results and observations

We compare three models: (1) **wavlm-semantic**, a single projection head trained for semantic similarity only; (2) **wavlm-multiaxis**, multiple projection heads trained jointly for semantic, speaker identity, gender, and dialect axes; and (3) **wavlm-resemblyzer**, a multiaxis variant where the speaker head target was replaced with embeddings from Resemblyzer, a pretrained speaker verification model.
Baselines are (a) a text similarity model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 applied to transcripts) and (b) CLAP [@elizalde2023clap] embeddings used directly for retrieval.

### Cross-speaker semantic recall (p315 → VCTK)

Retrieval of held-out VCTK speaker p315 against a VCTK index (R@k, hit = same sentence, different speaker):

| Model | R@1 | R@5 | R@10 | MRR |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| text baseline | 94.4% | 99.4% | 100% | 96.8% |
| wavlm-semantic | 90.0% | 96.2% | 96.2% | 92.7% |
| wavlm-multiaxis | 51.9% | 72.5% | 81.2% | 61.1% |
| wavlm-resemblyzer | ~0% | ~0% | ~0% | ~0% |
| CLAP baselines | 0% | 0% | 0% | — |

The semantic model reaches 90% R@1, close to the text ceiling of 94.4%.
The multiaxis model drops to 52%: the speaker axis attracts same-sounding training speakers, overriding the semantic signal.
The resemblyzer variant essentially fails: the speaker head from a pretrained verification model dominates and the semantic axis loses effective influence on retrieval.
CLAP embeddings produce no same-sentence hits, confirming that audio-level content similarity does not capture sentence identity in this setting.

### Cross-corpus preference-flip (rehasp / OSR)

We evaluate on a mixed index containing both OSR and rehasp recordings of the Harvard sentences.
Because rehasp provides many within-speaker recordings of the same sentence, naive semantic similarity tends to retrieve same-speaker rehasp variants before cross-corpus OSR matches.
We report P@k, where a hit is correct if it comes from OSR (different corpus from the rehasp query) and reads the same sentence.

| Model | Index condition | P@1 | P@5 |
|---|---|---:|---:|
| wavlm-semantic | OSR-only | 63.9% | 76.8% |
| wavlm-multiaxis | OSR-only | 16.8% | 30.1% |
| wavlm-resemblyzer | OSR-only | 3.2% | 5.3% |
| wavlm-multiaxis | mixed, `spk` = +1 | 0.4% | 13.3% |
| wavlm-multiaxis | mixed, `spk` = −1 | 0.3% | 12.5% |

With the mixed index, correct OSR matches are buried behind same-speaker rehasp variants regardless of speaker weight, indicating that the current model's speaker axis does not yet produce sufficient disentanglement for the preference-flip to be effective.
Without the same-speaker distractor (OSR-only index), wavlm-semantic retrieves the correct sentence 64% of the time, establishing a useful ceiling for the semantic axis alone.
We report a controlled ablation of axis configuration and dimension choices in the following section.

We also considered gender as an axis, motivated by its common use as an annotation category and its relevance for user-controlled retrieval.
In many applications, users may wish to constrain or vary results according to speaker characteristics such as gender.
However, in practice it proved to be a weak factor: it is low-cardinality and strongly correlated with speaker identity, making it difficult to separate into an independent similarity axis.

This can be contrasted with factors such as timbre, which have been the focus of prior disentanglement work and can be partially separated under constrained settings.
However, such factors are less clearly aligned with user-facing retrieval needs.
More broadly, this highlights a practical tension: attributes that are amenable to representation learning are not always those that users wish to control, while user-relevant attributes may be difficult to isolate in practice.
We therefore focus on axes that balance both considerations, namely semantic content, speaker identity, and dialect.

### Ablation: axis configuration and dimension choices

To isolate the effects of speaker axis dimensionality, speaker teacher choice, and semantic dimension reduction, we trained eight model variants in a controlled run.
All models use `semantic:384` (matching the MiniLM teacher exactly, eliminating any alignment matrix on the semantic axis), except for the PCA baseline which uses `semantic:256` targets derived by PCA projection of the MiniLM embedding space.
No gender axis is included in any variant.

**Semantic retrieval** (p315 → VCTK, cross-speaker semantic recall):

| Model | Speaker axis | R@1 | R@5 | R@10 |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| text baseline | — | 94.4% | 99.4% | 100% |
| wavlm-sem384 | — | 89.3% | 96.1% | 97.5% |
| wavlm-sem256-pca | — | 87.1% | 95.3% | 97.1% |
| wavlm-sem384-spk256-resem-grl | resemblyzer + GRL | 85.9% | 94.7% | 96.8% |
| wavlm-sem384-spk256-xvec-pca | x-vector PCA(256) | 82.4% | 92.8% | 95.6% |
| wavlm-sem384-spk256-resem | resemblyzer (256-d) | 81.6% | 92.4% | 95.2% |
| wavlm-sem384-spk256-resem-dial | resemblyzer + dialect | 80.2% | 91.6% | 94.7% |
| wavlm-sem384-spk256-xvec | x-vector + align (256→768) | 76.3% | 88.9% | 93.1% |
| wavlm-sem384-spk768-xvec | x-vector (768-d) | 58.7% | 76.2% | 84.4% |

The semantic-only baseline (`wavlm-sem384`) confirms that removing the alignment matrix from the semantic axis recovers performance consistent with the text ceiling.
The PCA-compressed variant (`wavlm-sem256-pca`) falls only 2.2 points below sem384 at R@1, indicating that the top 256 principal components of the MiniLM embedding space retain nearly all discriminative content; the remaining gap likely reflects information genuinely absent in the compressed representation rather than training noise.

The x-vector dimension configuration has a substantial effect on semantic quality.
The 768-d variant (`spk768-xvec`) drops to 58.7% R@1: the speaker axis dominates the combined similarity by raw dimensionality, effectively overriding the semantic signal.
Reducing the x-vector targets to 256-d via PCA (`spk256-xvec-pca`) largely recovers semantic quality (82.4%), comparable to the resemblyzer variant (81.6%) despite using a different speaker teacher.
The alignment-matrix variant (`spk256-xvec`), which trains a 256-d projection against the full 768-d x-vector targets via a fixed random orthogonal alignment, degrades noticeably relative to the PCA variant (76.3% vs 82.4%), confirming that the projection introduces more noise than the dimension reduction itself.

Adding dialect supervision (`resem-dial`) slightly degrades semantic quality relative to the plain resemblyzer variant, consistent with the observation that the dialect axis learns rapidly and may capture speaker-discriminative signal that leaks into the combined similarity score.
Gradient reversal (`resem-grl`) partially compensates: by actively penalising speaker-correlated signal in the semantic subspace during training, it recovers approximately 4 points of R@1 relative to the plain resemblyzer variant, at the cost of somewhat reduced speaker axis sharpness.

**Preference-flip** (rehasp queries, OSR+rehasp mixed index):

All four resemblyzer and PCA x-vector variants (768-d omitted as the semantic axis is effectively disabled in that model) show a clear preference reversal when the speaker weight sign is flipped.
Mean rank of query-index pairs by category (lower = retrieved earlier):

| Model | Weights (sem, spk) | same sent / same spk | same sent / diff spk | diff sent / same spk | diff sent / diff spk |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| sem384 (reference) | (1, 0) | 14.2 | 14.9 | 22.6 | 28.1 |
| spk256-resem | (1, +1) | 9.3 | 17.4 | 18.2 | 31.7 |
| spk256-resem | (1, −1) | 19.8 | 9.1 | 28.4 | 27.1 |
| spk256-resem-grl | (1, +1) | 8.7 | 17.9 | 19.1 | 32.1 |
| spk256-resem-grl | (1, −1) | 21.4 | 7.3 | 30.7 | 26.8 |
| spk256-resem-dial | (1, +1) | 9.1 | 17.1 | 18.8 | 31.8 |
| spk256-resem-dial | (1, −1) | 20.3 | 8.8 | 29.2 | 27.4 |
| spk256-xvec-pca | (1, +1) | 9.8 | 17.2 | 18.5 | 31.6 |
| spk256-xvec-pca | (1, −1) | 20.1 | 9.4 | 28.9 | 27.3 |

Under positive speaker weight, same-sentence/same-speaker items are retrieved earliest (lowest rank), as the shared speaker signal reinforces the semantic match.
Under negative speaker weight, same-sentence/different-speaker items (cross-corpus OSR hits) move to rank 7–9, the best rank of any category — confirming that the speaker axis, when inverted, actively suppresses same-speaker retrieval and surfaces content-matched cross-corpus utterances.
The gradient-reversal variant shows the sharpest flip: the same-sentence/different-speaker rank under negative weight drops to 7.3 compared with 9.1 for the plain resemblyzer variant, while the same-sentence/same-speaker rank rises to 21.4.
The pure semantic baseline shows no flip, as expected: mean ranks for same-sentence/same-speaker and same-sentence/different-speaker are nearly equal (14.2 vs 14.9) and are unaffected by speaker weight.

## Conclusion

We presented a factor-partitioned embedding framework for speech that supports similarity search across multiple attribute axes simultaneously.
Axis-specific projection heads are trained via teacher distillation or contrastive objectives over shared-label pairs, producing a single concatenated embedding that can be queried with signed per-axis weights.
Experiments on cross-corpus retrieval demonstrate that negative axis weighting can suppress same-speaker bias and surface semantically matched utterances across recording conditions.

The ablation confirms several practical conclusions.
First, matching the projection dimension to the teacher dimension eliminates alignment matrix noise and is the single most reliable way to preserve semantic quality; PCA compression to 256 dimensions incurs only a small penalty (~2 points R@1) and is preferable to any learned projection when the target dimension is smaller than the teacher.
Second, the speaker axis dimension should be matched to the semantic axis dimension; a 768-d speaker axis with a 384-d semantic axis effectively disables the semantic signal in the combined similarity.
Third, gradient reversal provides a measurable improvement in semantic quality when a speaker axis is present, without substantially reducing the speaker axis's utility for the preference-flip task.
Fourth, the preference-flip mechanism is robust across speaker teacher choices: resemblyzer and PCA-reduced x-vector targets produce comparable flip magnitudes, and dialect supervision adds marginal value for retrieval control beyond speaker identity alone.

A natural next step is to extend the framework to larger and more varied read-speech collections, such as LibriVox, where additional axes including prosody and speaking style can be explored alongside wider variation in speakers, accents, and content.
Future work will also investigate stronger factor separation objectives and more principled evaluation of axis specialisation.
One promising direction for the speaker axis is to adopt a within-utterance positive-pair construction [@deng2024ctvc]: sampling two segments from the same utterance as positives enforces a stricter time-invariance constraint than cross-utterance speaker pairs, since content variation within an utterance is minimal.
